153	## File Templates
154	
155	### Template 1: Shebang and Copyright
156	
157	```bash
158	#!/bin/bash
159	# shellcheck disable=SC2153  # kindle chain - per BCG
160	#
161	# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
162	#
163	# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
164	# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
165	# You may obtain a copy of the License at
166	#
167	#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
168	#
169	# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
170	# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
171	# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
172	# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
173	# limitations under the License.
174	#
175	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
176	#
177	```
178	
179	**Line-2 directive (REQUIRED for cross-module consumers).** Files that reference cross-module `Z*_/RB*_` readonly state — full BCG modules and BCG-adjacent bootstrappers alike — MUST include `# shellcheck disable=SC2153  # kindle chain - per BCG` on line 2, immediately after the shebang and before any other content. The coda wording is fixed; do not vary.
180	
181	Placement is load-bearing. shellcheck honors file-scope directives only when they precede every executable statement; placing the directive after `set -euo pipefail` or `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` reduces it to next-statement scope and the disable becomes silently inert — warnings continue to fire. The `set -u` clause of `set -euo pipefail` is the runtime backstop for the same typo class SC2153 catches statically: misspelled cross-module references die at first reference rather than silently expanding to empty.
182	
183	### Tabtarget Path Indirection
184	
185	A tabtarget in `tt/` is a colophon, not a program — it names *what* to run, never *how* to reach it. A single trampoline, `tt/z-launcher.sh`, owns the "how." Every other tabtarget is a declarative stub: a shebang, a `BURD_*` config block, and a byte-identical `exec` into the trampoline.
186	
187	```bash
188	#!/bin/bash
189	export BURD_LAUNCHER=launcher.rbw_workbench.sh
190	exec "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/z-launcher.sh" "${0##*/}" "${@}"
191	```
192	
193	The exec line carries no launcher token — it is identical, character for character, across every tabtarget. All per-tabtarget variation lives in the `BURD_*` block between the shebang and the exec: which launcher (`BURD_LAUNCHER`, always the first config line), and any dispatch flags (`BURD_NO_LOG`, `BURD_INTERACTIVE`) that follow.
194	
195	The trampoline has exactly two responsibilities:
196	
197	1. **Resolve the moorings location.** The consumer's configuration tree (launchers, regimes) lives under a moorings directory whose path the trampoline holds as a single hardcoded literal. This is the single point of customization at the tabtarget layer — the parallel to BURC at the CLI layer. Move the moorings, edit one line; every tabtarget follows.
198	2. **Normalize cwd to repo root.** The trampoline `cd`s to the repo root before dispatching, so every workbench starts from a deterministic directory regardless of where the user invoked the tabtarget. Downstream code relies on a known cwd rather than reconstructing one.
199	
200	**The launcher name.** `BURD_LAUNCHER` is a bare launcher basename — `launcher.<id>_workbench.sh`. Every launcher co-locates in the moorings launcher directory (`rbml_launchers/`), so the trampoline resolves the basename directly against that one directory and exec's it, forwarding the tabtarget basename and user args unchanged. `BURD_LAUNCHER` is not a private positional convention: it is a regime variable — declared in `burd_regime`, allowlisted in `bud_dispatch` — so the launcher identity travels the same validated `BURD_*` channel as every other dispatch value, and flows downstream unchanged.
201	
202	Why name the launcher in a config variable rather than pass it as a positional dispatch token? **Because the byte-identical exec line is load-bearing.** When the only thing that varies between two tabtargets is their `BURD_*` block, a tabtarget is purely declarative — it states *which launcher and which flags*, and holds no dispatch logic that can drift from the trampoline's expectations. The launcher identity rides the same validated `BURD_*` channel as every other dispatch value rather than a parallel argument slot, and the exec collapses to a true constant the qualifier checks against a single literal.
203	
204	This indirection is the tabtarget-layer instance of CLI as Module Gateway: a tabtarget is external code, and it reaches module functionality only through a launcher the trampoline resolves — never by sourcing or kindling directly. The launcher → dispatch chain it hands off to is what provides the Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables described next.
205	
206	### Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables
207	
208	Three BURD variables carry directory paths across the exec boundary into CLI processes. These are derived from BURC configuration and exported by bud_dispatch.sh during zbud_setup():
209	
210	| Variable | Source | Value |
211	|----------|--------|-------|
212	| `BURD_TOOLS_DIR` | `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | Project tools root directory |
213	| `BURD_BUK_DIR` | Derived from `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | BUK subdirectory (`$BURC_TOOLS_DIR/buk`) |
214	| `BURD_TABTARGET_DIR` | `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` | Tabtarget directory |
215	
216	These variables are available in exec'd CLI processes without requiring the CLI to re-source BURC. CLIs that need stable paths to tools or utilities should use these variables rather than reconstructing paths from `BASH_SOURCE`.
217	
218	### Template 2: CLI Entry Point
219	
220	BUD variables are provided by dispatch. The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other dependencies are sourced inside the furnish function. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime env vars — furnish gates after `buc_doc_env` calls before any sourcing that might fail.
221	
222	```bash
223	# «shebang»
224	# «copyright»
225	
226	set -euo pipefail
227	
228	source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
229	
230	z«prefix»_furnish() {
231	
232	  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
233	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
234	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
235	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
236	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
237	
238	  # Source all dependencies
239	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
240	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/«prefix»_«name».sh"
241	
242	  # Source regime files, if any
243	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"
244	
245	  z«prefix»_kindle
246	  z«prefix»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
247	}
248	
249	buc_execute «prefix»_ "«module_description_phrase»" z«prefix»_furnish "$@"
250	
251	# eof
252	```
253	
254	### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)
255	
256	The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.
257	
258	**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.
259	
260	```bash
261	z«prefix»_furnish() {
262	  local z_command="${1:-}"
263	
264	  # Document environment variables
265	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
266	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
267	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
268	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
269	
270	  # Always source light dependencies
271	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
272	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"
273	
274	  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
275	  case "${z_command}" in
276	    «prefix»_survey|«prefix»_audit)
277	      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
278	      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
279	      zheavy_kindle
280	      ;;
281	    «prefix»_validate|«prefix»_render)
282	      # These commands work with config only
283	      ;;
284	  esac
285	
286	  z«prefix»_kindle
287	}
288	```
289	
290	**Key points:**
291	- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "«prefix»_validate") as `$1`
292	- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
293	- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
294	- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
295	- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies
296	
297	**Benefits:**
298	- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
299	- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
300	- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
301	- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure
302	
303	### Template 3: Implementation Module
304	
305	```bash
306	# «shebang»
307	# «copyright»
308	
309	set -euo pipefail
310	
311	# Multiple inclusion detection
312	test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
313	Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1
314	
315	# Tinder constants (pure string literals or tinder-on-tinder composition — available at source time)
316	# «PREFIX»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
317	# «PREFIX»_some_prefix="prefix_"
318	
319	######################################################################
320	# Internal Functions (z«prefix»_*)
321	
322	z«prefix»_kindle() {
323	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
324	
325	  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
326	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
327	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
328	  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps
329	
330	  # Validate module specific environment
331	  buv_file_exists "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present
332	
333	  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
334	  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"
335	
336	  # Kindle constant (public — safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
337	  «PREFIX»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"
338	
339	  # Kindle constants (internal)
340	  Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
341	  Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_result.json"
342	  Z«PREFIX»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
343	  Z«PREFIX»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use
344	
345	  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
346	  Z«PREFIX»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"
347	
348	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
349	}
350	
351	z«prefix»_sentinel() {
352	  test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module «prefix» not kindled - call z«prefix»_kindle first"
353	}
354	
355	# Helper functions follow
356	
357	######################################################################
358	# External Functions («prefix»_*)
359	
360	«prefix»_«command»() {
361	  z«prefix»_sentinel
362	
363	  local z_param1="${1:-}"
364	  local z_param2="${2:-}"
365	
366	  # Documentation block
367	  buc_doc_brief "«one_line_description»"
368	  buc_doc_param "«required_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
369	  buc_doc_oparm "«optional_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
370	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
371	
372	  # Implementation
373	  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"
374	
375	  buc_step "«user_visible_milestone»"
376	  buc_log_args "«detailed_progress_information»"
377	  [command] || buc_die "«failure_explanation»"
378	
379	  buc_success "«success_message»"
380	}
381	```
382	
383	**Kindle constant — anti-pattern:**
384	
385	```bash
386	# ❌ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
387	z«prefix»_setup() {
388	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
389	}
390	
391	# ✅ Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
392	z«prefix»_kindle() {
393	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"
394	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
395	}
396	```
397	
398	**Tinder vs kindle constant — choosing correctly:**
399	
400	```bash
401	# ✅ Tinder constant: pure string, available at source time
402	«PREFIX»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name — no kindle needed
403	
404	# ✅ Tinder-on-tinder composition: expansion of earlier tinder constant
405	«PREFIX»_role_director="director"
406	«PREFIX»_fact_ext_director="ext-${«PREFIX»_role_director}"  # resolves at source time
407	
408	# ✅ Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
409	«PREFIX»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name — kindle required
410	
411	# ❌ Wrong: runtime variable expansion in tinder position
412	«PREFIX»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: BURD_TEMP_DIR is runtime — must be kindle
413	
414	# ❌ Wrong: computation in tinder position
415	«PREFIX»_color_red=$(z«prefix»_color '1;31')   # VIOLATION: $() — must be in kindle
416	
417	# ❌ Wrong: expansion of kindle constant in tinder position
418	«PREFIX»_derived="${«PREFIX»_KINDLED_VAR}/sub"  # VIOLATION: KINDLED_VAR available only after kindle
419	```
420	
421	## Variable Handling (General Rules)
422	
423	### Expansion Requirements
424	
425	**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
426	```bash
427	"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
428	"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
429	"${1:-}"                # Parameters with defaults
430	```
431	
432	**Never use:**
433	```bash
434	$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
435	"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
436	${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
437	```
438	
439	**No tilde in quoted strings** — tilde expansion is suppressed inside quotes:
440	```bash
441	# ❌ Tilde does not expand in quotes
442	"~/Downloads/file.txt"          # Literal ~ character, not home directory
443	
444	# ✅ Use $HOME instead
445	"${HOME}/Downloads/file.txt"    # Expands correctly
446	```
447	
448	**Quote inside parameter expansion operators:**
449	```bash
450	# ✅ Inner expansion quoted separately — matches literally
451	"${z_path%"${z_suffix}"}"       # Strip suffix
452	"${z_path#"${z_prefix}"}"       # Strip prefix
453	
454	# ❌ Inner expansion unquoted — matches as a glob pattern
455	"${z_path%${z_suffix}}"         # z_suffix with * or ? chars matches as glob!
456	"${z_path#${z_prefix}}"         # Same risk
457	```
458	
459	When using `%`, `%%`, `#`, `##` operators with a variable as the pattern, the inner expansion must be quoted separately. Without inner quotes, the pattern is glob-expanded — a suffix containing `*` or `?` silently matches more than intended.
460	
461	### Assignment Patterns
462	
463	```bash
464	# Direct assignment
465	local z_message="Processing item"
466	
467	# ❌ NEVER declare multiple variables on one line
468	local z_name z_value z_result  # Hidden initialization failures!
469	
470	# ✅ ALWAYS one declaration per line with explicit initialization
471	local z_name=""
472	local z_value="default"
473	local z_result=0
474	
475	# ❌ NEVER use local -i — silently coerces non-integer values to 0
476	local -i z_count="${z_input}"  # "abc" becomes 0 — violates no-silent-failures!
477	
478	# ✅ Use plain local with explicit validation
479	local z_count="${z_input}"
480	test "${z_count}" -ge 0 2>/dev/null || buc_die "z_count must be non-negative integer, got: ${z_count}"
481	
482	# ✅ Two-line pattern for captured values (explicit and debuggable)
483	local z_token
484	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
485	
486	# ❌ NEVER combine declaration with capture
487	local z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture)  # Hides exit status!
488	
489	# From file using builtin $(<file) - NOT command substitution, a bash builtin
490	local z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}")
491	test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}"
492	
493	# Never split declaration from $(<file) assignment
494	# ❌ local z_content
495	# ❌ z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_FILE}")  # Unnecessary verbosity
496	
497	# Use Capture functions for secrets that must never touch disk
498	local z_token
499	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
500	
501	# Temp files INSTEAD of command substitution holds intermediate results
502	echo "${z_result}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE}"
503	complex_command > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
504	process < "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
505	local z_result=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}")
506	test -n "${z_result}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}"
507	```
508	
509	### Prefer Bash Builtins Over External Tools
510	
511	```bash
512	# ✅ String manipulation with parameter expansion
513	"${z_path##*/}"                    # Instead of: basename
514	"${z_path%/*}"                     # Instead of: dirname
515	"${z_string//old/new}"             # Instead of: sed 's/old/new/g'
516	"${z_string#prefix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/^prefix//'
517	"${z_string%suffix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/suffix$//'
518	
519	# ✅ Reading lines with 'read' — guard handles missing trailing newline
520	while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
521	  echo "Processing: ${z_line}" || buc_die "Failed to echo line"
522	done < "${Z_MODULE_INPUT_FILE}"
523	
524	# ✅ Arithmetic with $((...))
525	z_result=$((z_value * 2 + 10))    # Instead of: expr or bc
526	```
527	
528	### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution
529	
530	All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.
531	
532	These rules govern **command substitution** — `$(command)`, which spawns a subshell, runs a command, and can fail. They do not apply to `$(<file)`, which despite sharing the `$()` syntax is a bash **builtin file redirect**: no subshell, no command, nothing to fail. `$(<file)` needs no `|| buc_die` guard — it takes content validation (`test -n` on the result) instead, since a successful read of an empty or missing file still yields the empty string.
533	
534	```bash
535	# ❌ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
536	z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!
537	
538	# ❌ Anti-pattern: unguarded command substitution
539	z_timestamp=$(date +%s)  # Failure is silent!
540	
541	# ✅ Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
542	complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
543	process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
544	read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"
545	
546	# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
547	date +%s > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed to get timestamp"
548	local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
549	test -n "${z_timestamp}" || buc_die "Empty timestamp"
550	
551	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
552	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)
553	```
554	
555	**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.
556	
557	### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress
558	
559	Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.
560	
561	```bash
562	# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
563	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
564	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"
565	
566	# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
567	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
568	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
569	```
570	
571	**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.
572	
573	**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:
574	
575	```bash
576	# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
577	Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_"
578	
579	# In command function — integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
580	local z_index=0
581	while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
582	  local z_manifest_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
583	  local z_stderr_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"
584	
585	  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
586	    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
587	    || buc_die "Failed to inspect ${z_image}:${z_tag} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
588	
589	  z_index=$((z_index + 1))
590	done
591	```
592	
593	### String Usage
594	
595	```bash
596	# Single-line HERE-string allowed for piping variables
597	jq '.field' <<<"${z_json_data}"
598	grep "pattern" <<<"${z_content}"
599	
600	# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
601	```
602	
603	### Readonly Patterns
604	
605	Readonly enforcement makes mutation bugs loud at the point of violation rather than silent cascades downstream. Three patterns at different scopes:
606	
607	#### `local -r` — Default for Local Variables
608	
609	Use `local -r` for every local variable that is assigned once and never modified. This is the majority of locals in a typical BCG function.
610	
611	```bash
612	# ✅ Default: readonly locals
613	local -r z_filepath="${1:-}"
614	local -r z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_idx]}"
615	local -r z_full_ref="${z_image}@${z_digest}"
616	local -r z_content=$(<"${z_file}")    # $(<file) is a builtin — safe on one line
617	```
618	
619	**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:
620	
621	```bash
622	# Exception 1: Loop counters
623	local z_index=0
624	z_index=$((z_index + 1))
625	
626	# Exception 2: Per-iteration synthesized variables (reassigned each pass)
627	local z_manifest_file=""
628	for z_spec in "${z_specs[@]}"; do
629	  z_manifest_file="${Z_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
630	  ...
631	done
632	
633	# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
634	local z_token
635	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
636	
637	# Exception 4: Accumulators and state-tracking flags
638	local z_any_failed=0
639	local z_current_group=""
640	```
641	
642	**Why not `local -i`?** `local -i` silently coerces non-integers to 0 — it hides bugs. `local -r` does the opposite — it surfaces mutation bugs loudly. Same flag syntax, opposite safety properties.
643	
644	#### `readonly` — Every Kindle Constant
645	
646	Every variable assigned in a kindle function is a constant. Apply `readonly` to every assignment — whether the value is a literal, computed from other variables, or composed from prior assignments:
647	
648	```bash
649	z«prefix»_kindle() {
650	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
651	
652	  # Simple kindle constants (literal values, but defined in kindle for readonly enforcement)
653	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_API_VERSION="v2"
654	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_MAX_RETRIES=3
655	
656	  # Computed from already-set values
657	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_REGISTRY_HOST="${RBGD_LOCATION}${RBGC_HOST_SUFFIX}"
658	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
659	
660	  # Composed from prior kindle assignments
661	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_ACCEPT_TYPES="${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_A},${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_B}"
662	
663	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
664	}
665	```
666	
667	If a kindle function uses intermediate variables to build up a result (e.g., composing array elements), the final assignment is still `readonly`. Intermediates that are truly local to the build-up should use `local -r`.
668	
669	**The KINDLED sentinel** is a kindle constant — it gets `readonly` like every other kindle assignment. The `test -z` guard at the top of kindle fires before any reassignment attempt, so `readonly` is belt-and-suspenders: the guard gives a friendly error message, `readonly` is the backstop.
670	
671	#### Mutable Kindle State
672	
673	Some kindle functions initialize mutable state alongside constants — counters, accumulators, registry rolls, and builder state that are modified after kindle returns. These variables **must not** be `readonly`, but they must be visually distinguishable from kindle constants.
674	
675	**Convention**: Mutable kindle state uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name`. Constants use **UPPERCASE** `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`. Case alone signals mutability.
676	
677	```bash
678	z«prefix»_kindle() {
679	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
680	
681	  # ✅ Kindle constants — UPPERCASE, readonly
682	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
683	
684	  # ✅ Mutable kindle state — lowercase, NO readonly
685	  z_«prefix»_file_index=0              # counter incremented post-kindle
686	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()              # registry populated by enroll calls
687	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""           # builder state set during enrollment
688	
689	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
690	}
691	```
692	
693	**Rules**:
694	- Mutable kindle state follows the same `z_` internal prefix as roll arrays
695	- Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state
696	- If a variable is initialized in kindle and never mutated afterward, it is a constant — use `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`
697	- The `_roll` suffix is conventional for parallel-array registries but not required for all mutable state (counters, flags, builder state use descriptive names)
698	
699	**Test reset**: When tests need fresh enrollment state, provide an internal reset function that clears mutable kindle state without touching `KINDLED`. Re-kindling a module is never correct — the sentinel is `readonly`.
700	
701	```bash
702	# ✅ Test support: reset mutable state, module stays kindled
703	z«prefix»_reset_enrollment() {
704	  z«prefix»_sentinel
705	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()
706	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""
707	  # ... clear all mutable kindle state ...
708	}
709	```
710	
711	**The re-source trap** — `readonly` belongs in code, never in sourceable `.env` files:
712	```bash
713	# ❌ NEVER in sourceable .env files
714	# If a .env file contains `readonly FOO=bar`, sourcing it a second time
715	# (e.g., regime reload, test re-initialization) dies with:
716	#   bash: FOO: readonly variable
717	```
718	
719	#### `readonly VAR` — Lock After Enforce
720	
721	For regime variables loaded from `.env` files, lock them *after* validation succeeds. This is the regime archetype's lock step (see Regime Module Archetype above):
722	
723	```bash
724	z«prefix»_enforce() {
725	  z«prefix»_sentinel
726	  buv_vet «SCOPE»
727	  # ... custom format checks ...
728	}
729	
730	# In the lock step (called after enforce succeeds):
731	# Lock all enrolled variables — any downstream mutation is now a loud error
732	readonly REGI_FIELD_ONE REGI_FIELD_TWO REGI_FIELD_THREE
733	
734	# Derived state built from validated values:
735	Z«PREFIX»_DOCKER_ENV=("-e" "REGI_FIELD_ONE=${REGI_FIELD_ONE}")
736	```
737	
738	**Ordering matters**: Build derived state (docker env arrays, rollup strings) *after* enforce but *before* or *during* the lock call. Once variables are readonly, derived state captures validated, immutable values.
739	
740	## Special Function Definitions
741	
742	### Special Function Patterns
743	
744	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
745	
746	| Type      | Pattern                        | First Line           | Returns                      | buc_step                  | Contract                            |
747	|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
748	| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |
749	| Capture   | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout once at end or exit 1 | No (use buc_log_«source») | Clean error handling, single return |
750	| Enroll    | `[z]«prefix»_[«scope»_]enroll` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `z_«funcname»_«retval»` vars | No (use buc_log_«source») | Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) in kindle only; returns via variables |
751	| Recite    | `[z]«prefix»_«what»_recite`    | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout or exit 1             | No (use buc_log_«source») | Read-only access to rolls; never mutates |
752	| Fixture baste | `z«tb»_«name»_baste`         | —                   | — (side effects only)        | Optional                  | Fixture subshell; kindle, source, configure |
753	| Tcase     | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`           | —                    | — (exit status to suite)     | No                        | Case subshell; assertions, verification |
754	| Yawp      | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_yawp`     | `z«module»_sentinel` | `z_«module»_«group»` var    | No                        | Pure assignment, cannot fail, no output |
755	
756	---
757	
758	#### Capture Functions (controlled command substitution)
759	
760	**Purpose: Return a single processed value through stdout with clean error handling.**
761	
762	Capture functions encapsulate command substitution to ensure predictable error handling and single-point-of-return semantics.
763	
764	```bash
765	z«prefix»_«name»_capture() {
766	  z«prefix»_sentinel
767	
768	  # Process and validate internally
769	  local z_result
770	  z_result=$(some_command | process) || return 1
771	
772	  # Validate without buc_die
773	  test -n "${z_result}" || return 1
774	
775	  # Single output at end
776	  echo "${z_result}"
777	}
778	
779	# Caller uses two-line pattern
780	local z_value
781	z_value=$(z«prefix»_«name»_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture value"
782	```
783	
784	**Use capture functions for:**
785	- Secrets that must never touch disk
786	- Values requiring command substitution or pipeline processing
787	- Any computed value where clean error handling matters
788	
789	**Contract:**
790	- Returns stdout once at end or exits with status 1
791	- Never uses `buc_die` internally
792	- No side effects beyond the returned value
793	- May source credential files when necessary for security
794	- May use `buc_log_«source»` for forensic trail (writes to transcript file only)
795	- Never writes secrets to disk
796	- Never writes to stderr
797	
798	#### Predicate Functions (status only, no output)
799	
800	Predicate functions are used to make decisions, not indicating error.
801	
802	```bash
803	z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate() {
804	  z«prefix»_sentinel
805	
806	  local z_file="${1:-}"
807	
808	  # Return status only - no output, no dies
809	  test -f "${z_file}" || return 1
810	  grep -q "required_pattern" "${z_file}" || return 1
811	
812	  return 0
813	}
814	
815	# Caller uses in conditionals
816	z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate "${z_config}" || buc_warn "Config invalid, using defaults"
817	```
818	
819	#### Enroll Functions (kindle-only registry population)
820	
821	**Purpose**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during module initialization. Enroll functions solve the subshell problem: when a function both mutates shared state and returns a value, calling it in `$()` loses the mutations.
822	
823	**The parallel-array registry pattern (rolls)**:
824	- Kindle initializes N empty arrays (rolls) of the same length
825	- Enroll validates inputs and appends atomically to all rolls
826	- Recite functions (see below) provide read-only access after kindle completes
827	- Variable naming: `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll` for all roll arrays (internal state, initialized in kindle)
828	
829	**Example** (using synthetic notation — «reg» owns the rolls, «con» consumes them):
830	
831	```bash
832	# Module «reg» — owns the rolls
833	z«reg»_kindle() {
834	  test -z "${Z«REG»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
835	
836	  z_«reg»_name_roll=()
837	  z_«reg»_target_roll=()
838	  z_«reg»_handler_roll=()
839	
840	  Z«REG»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last — sentinel guards all subsequent calls
841	}
842	
843	# Enroll function — called by consuming module's kindle, NOT «reg»'s own kindle
844	«reg»_enroll() {
845	  z«reg»_sentinel   # Safe: «reg» is already kindled when consumer calls this
846	
847	  local z_name="${1:-}"
848	  local z_target="${2:-}"
849	  local z_handler="${3:-}"
850	  test -n "${z_name}" || buc_die "enroll: name required"
851	
852	  # Registration-time validation
853	  declare -F "${z_handler}" >/dev/null || buc_die "enroll: handler not found: ${z_handler}"
854	
855	  z_«reg»_name_roll+=("${z_name}")
856	  z_«reg»_target_roll+=("${z_target}")
857	  z_«reg»_handler_roll+=("${z_handler}")
858	
859	  # Return via variable — NOT echo
860	  z_«reg»_enroll_name="${z_name}"
861	}
862	
863	# Module «con» — consumes the registry
864	z«con»_kindle() {
865	  test -z "${Z«CON»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
866	
867	  # «reg» must be kindled first (rolls initialized, sentinel passes)
868	  «reg»_enroll "alpha" "/path/alpha" "handle_alpha"
869	  Z«CON»_DEFAULT="${z_«reg»_enroll_name}"
870	
871	  «reg»_enroll "bravo" "/path/bravo" "handle_bravo"
872	
873	  Z«CON»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last
874	}
875	```
876	
877	**Key constraints**:
878	- Enroll functions may ONLY be called within kindle
879	- Rolls are initialized in kindle, populated by enroll in kindle, then immutable after kindle completes
880	- Enroll functions work exclusively with rolls (parallel arrays) — no other shared state mutation
881	- Registration-time validation: check invariants at enroll time, not at recite/execution time
882	- Return values use `z_«funcname»_«retval»` convention (function name embedded verbatim for traceability)
883	- Must NOT be called inside `$()` — side effects would be lost
884	- May use `buc_die` internally (unlike `_capture`)
885	
886	**Two-level registries**: When entities have parent-child relationships (e.g., groups containing items), use two flat registries with a foreign-key column rather than per-parent dynamic arrays. This avoids `eval` and stays bash 3.2 safe.
887	
888	**Contract summary**:
889	- Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) only
890	- Returns value(s) via `z_«funcname»_«retval»` variables (NOT echo)
891	- Called exclusively within kindle
892	- Should rarely be used — only when a function must both mutate and return
893	
894	#### Recite Functions (read-only roll access)
895	
896	**Purpose: Provide read-only access to roll arrays populated by enroll functions.**
897	
898	Recite functions are the only access path to rolls after kindle completes. They must never mutate roll arrays.
899	
900	```bash
901	«prefix»_«what»_recite() {
902	  z«prefix»_sentinel
903	
904	  local z_key="${1:-}"
905	  test -n "${z_key}" || return 1
906	
907	  local z_i=0
908	  for z_i in "${!z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
909	    test "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_key}" || continue
910	    echo "${z_«prefix»_target_roll[$z_i]}" || return 1
911	    return 0
912	  done
913	  return 1
914	}
915	
916	# Caller uses two-line capture pattern
917	local z_target
918	z_target=$(«prefix»_target_recite "alpha") || buc_die "not found"
919	```
920	
921	**Contract:**
922	- Read-only: MUST NOT mutate roll arrays or any shared state
923	- Returns via echo (like capture functions) — safe to call in `$()`
924	- Returns 1 on not-found (like `_capture`) — never uses `buc_die` internally
925	- Caller decides error handling: `|| buc_die` or conditional
926	- Usable anywhere after kindle completes (not restricted to kindle)
927	
928	#### Yawp Functions (pure-computation group return)
929	
930	**Purpose: Produce a value into a shared group return variable via pure bash assignment. No subshells, no stdout, no stderr.**
931	
932	Named for Whitman's "barbaric yawp" — a loud, unmistakeable declaration into a known place. Yawp functions solve the high-frequency value-production problem: when dozens of calls per function each need a computed string, `$()` subshell overhead is disproportionate to the work being done.
933	
934	```bash
935	# «prefix»_«name»_yawp arg1 [arg2] — what it stamps
936	# → local -r z_name="${z_«module»_«group»}"
937	«prefix»_«name»_yawp() {
938	  z«module»_sentinel
939	  z_«module»_«group»="${ZMOD_PREFIX}${1:-}${ZMOD_SUFFIX}"
940	}
941	```
942	
943	The two-line comment is the per-function template: signature on line 1, mandatory capture pattern on line 2. The `→` arrow is a visual reminder that the caller must capture on the same line.
944	
945	**Return variable**: `z_«module»_«group»` — mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle. One per function group. All `_yawp` functions in a group share the same return variable. The value is valid until the next `_yawp` call in the same group.
946	
947	```bash
948	# In kindle — initialize mutable kindle state for yawp group
949	z_«module»_«group»=""
950	
951	# In function — pure assignment, no process spawning
952	«prefix»_cmd_yawp() {
953	  z«module»_sentinel
954	  z_«module»_«group»="${ZMOD_MARKER_CMD}${1:-}${ZMOD_MARKER_END}"
955	}
956	
957	# Caller pattern — semicolon capture on same line
958	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
959	«prefix»_ui_yawp "Save";         local -r z_ui="${z_«module»_«group»}"
960	```
961	
962	**Contract:**
963	- Sets `z_«module»_«group»` (mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle)
964	- **Caller MUST capture to `local -r` on the same line** — semicolon-separated, value is destroyed by the next `_yawp` call in the same group
965	- Pure bash builtins only — no external commands, no process spawning
966	- Cannot fail — no `|| buc_die` needed at call site
967	- Never uses `buc_die` internally
968	- No stdout, no stderr
969	- First line: sentinel check
970	
971	**Capture discipline — the return variable is a transient slot, not a durable value:**
972	
973	```bash
974	# CORRECT — semicolon capture on same line, compose from captured locals
975	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
976	«prefix»_tt_yawp "rbw-rrv";      local -r z_tt="${z_«module»_«group»}"
977	buh_line "Run ${z_cmd} to validate: ${z_tt}"
978	
979	# AVOID — two-line form works but doesn't enforce discipline
980	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
981	local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
982	# ↑ Nothing prevents inserting code between yawp and capture.
983	# The semicolon form makes interleaving structurally impossible.
984	
985	# WRONG — z_«group» used directly in output (fragile, unreadable)
986	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
987	buh_line "Run ${z_«module»_«group»}"
988	
989	# WRONG — two yawps before capture (first value lost)
990	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
991	«prefix»_tt_yawp "rbw-rrv"
992	buh_line "${z_«module»_«group»}"  # only has tt, cmd is gone
993	```
994	
995	The semicolon makes the capture discipline visually self-enforcing — you cannot accidentally insert code between the yawp and its capture. The two-line form invited interleaving mistakes; the one-line form eliminates them structurally.
996	
997	The one-yawp-one-capture rhythm is the invariant. Pre-captured constants (e.g., `RBYC_*` from `zrbyc_kindle`) are safe to interpolate directly because they were captured at kindle time — the discipline applies at the yawp call site, not at the interpolation site.
998	
999	**Use yawp functions for:**
1000	- Pure string assembly (concatenation, marker stamping)
1001	- Value production where `$()` subshell overhead is unnecessary
1002	- High-frequency call sites (dozens of calls per function)
1003	- Any computed value that uses only bash builtins and cannot fail
1004	
1005	**Do NOT use yawp functions for:**
1006	- Functions that can fail → use `_capture`
1007	- Functions with side effects beyond the return variable → use regular functions
1008	- Functions that mutate rolls → use `_enroll`
1009	- Functions that call external commands → use `_capture` or temp files
1010	
1011	---
1012	
1013	## When to Use Special Functions
1014	
1015	- **_capture**: Need a value that requires command substitution or clean error handling
1016	- **_predicate**: Need true/false for conditional logic without dying
1017	- **_enroll**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during kindle with validated inputs and return values
1018	- **_recite**: Read-only access to roll arrays populated by enroll functions
1019	- **_baste**: Fixture preparation inside fixture subshell (kindle, source, configure)
1020	- **_tcase**: Test case verification function inside `_tcase` subshell
1021	- **_yawp**: Pure-computation value production via group return variable. High-frequency call sites where `$()` overhead is disproportionate. String assembly, marker stamping, path construction.
1022	- **Neither**: Use temp files for multi-step pipelines, direct `|| buc_die` for simple failures
1023	
1024	## Naming Convention Patterns
1025	
1026	### ✅ Correct Patterns
1027	
1028	| Element                      | Pattern                      | Example                      | Location | Name/ Case Constraints        |
1029	|------------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|----------|-------------------------------|
1030	| Module prefix                | `[a-z]{2,4}_`                | `rbv_`, `buc_`, `auth_`      | Both     | single char group             |
1031	| Implementation file          | `«prefix»_«name».sh`         | `rbv_podman.sh`              | N/A      | lowercase (prefer 1 word, allow snake_case) |
1032	| CLI file                     | `«prefix»_cli.sh`            | `rbv_cli.sh`                 | N/A      | snake_case (fixed)            |
1033	| Public functions             | `«prefix»_«command»`         | `rbv_init`, `rbv_start`      | Impl     | snake_case (usually one word) |
1034	| Internal functions           | `z«prefix»_«name»`           | `zrbv_validate_pat`          | Impl     | snake_case (often multi word) |
1035	| Furnish function             | `z«prefix»_furnish`          | `zrbv_furnish`               | CLI      | fixed name                    |
1036	| Kindle function              | `z«prefix»_kindle`           | `zrbv_kindle`                | Impl     | fixed name                    |
1037	| Sentinel function            | `z«prefix»_sentinel`         | `zrbv_sentinel`              | Impl     | fixed name                    |
1038	| Predicate Internal functions | `z«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `zrbv_file_exists_predicate` | Impl     | snake_case                    |
1039	| Capture Internal functions   | `z«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `zrbv_get_token_capture`     | Impl     | snake_case                    |
1040	| Capture Public functions     | `«prefix»_«name»_capture`    | `rbv_get_token_capture`      | Impl     | snake_case                    |
1041	| Kindle constant (internal)   | `Z«PREFIX»_«NAME»`           | `ZRBV_TEMP_FILE`             | Impl     | SCREAMING_SNAKE (multi-word)  |
1042	| Kindle constant (public)     | `«PREFIX»_«NAME»`            | `RBV_REGIME_FILE`            | Both     | SCREAMING_SNAKE (multi-word)  |
1043	| Tinder constant (public)     | `«PREFIX»_«name»`            | `RBCC_rbrr_file`             | Impl     | lower_snake (multi-word)      |
1044	| Local parameters             | `z_«name»`                   | `z_vm_name`, `z_force_flag`  | Both     | snake_case (multi-word)       |
1045	| Enroll functions             | `[z]«prefix»_[«scope»_]enroll` | `«prefix»_enroll`          | Impl     | kindle-only                   |
1046	| Recite functions             | `[z]«prefix»_«what»_recite`  | `«prefix»_target_recite`     | Impl     | read-only, never mutates      |
1047	| Roll arrays                  | `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll`     | `z_rbv_target_roll`          | Impl     | snake_case, kindle-only       |
1048	| Enroll return vars           | `z_«funcname»_«retval»`      | `z_«prefix»_enroll_name`     | Impl     | snake_case (func name verbatim)|
1049	| Testbench file               | `«prefix»tb_testbench.sh`    | `rbtb_testbench.sh`          | N/A      | fixed name                    |
1050	| Test case file               | `«prefix»tc«xx»_«Name».sh`  | `rbtckk_KickTires.sh`        | N/A      | PascalCase name               |
1051	| Fixture baste function       | `z«tb»_«name»_baste`        | `zrbtb_ark_baste`            | Testbench| snake_case, fixture layer     |
1052	| Case function                | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`         | `rbtckk_false_tcase`         | Test case| snake_case, case layer        |
1053	| Yawp function (public)       | `«prefix»_«name»_yawp`      | `buyy_cmd_yawp`              | Impl     | snake_case                    |
1054	| Yawp function (internal)     | `z«prefix»_«name»_yawp`     | `zbuyf_resolve_yawp`         | Impl     | snake_case                    |
1055	| Yawp return variable         | `z_«module»_«group»`        | `z_buym_yelp`                | Impl     | snake_case, mutable kindle state |
1056	
1057	---
1058	
1059	## Runtime Visibility vs Comments
1060	
1061	### ✅ Use Runtime Visibility
1062	
1063	```bash
1064	# ✅ Use Runtime Visibility
1065	buc_step "Fetching manifest from registry"          # User milestones
1066	buc_log_args "Processing platform linux/amd64"      # Variable details
1067	jq '.response' file.json | buc_log_pipe            # Pipeline output
1068	
1069	# When to use each source:
1070	buc_log_args - Simple messages, variable values, progress markers
1071	buc_log_pipe - Command output, JSON responses, multi-line data
1072	```
1073	
1074	### ❌ Never Use Comments for Runtime Info
1075	
1076	```bash
1077	# Building JWT header         ❌ Wrong
1078	buc_log_args "Building JWT header"  ✅ Correct
1079	
1080	# Process each manifest       ❌ Wrong
